sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Altered level of consciousness (ALOC) refers to any change from a patient’s baseline alertness, orientation, or responsiveness. It may range from mild confusion to complete unresponsiveness (coma). ALOC is not a diagnosis but a symptom of an underlying condition which can include metabolic disturbances (hypo/hyperglycaemia), hypoxia, head injuries, CVA/TIA, seizures, intoxication (drugs, alcohol), infection, shock or toxins.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Reduced alertness or responsiveness, patient may be confused, dizzy or light-headed, drowsy, or unrousable
- Disorientated with inability to recognise time, place, or people
- Abnormal behaviour, anxious, restless, sleepy, sleep like state or comatose
- Inability to follow commands, may be unable to respond to verbal or painful stimuli
- Abnormal motor responses and may include posturing, seizures, or flaccidity
- Pupillary abnormalities, abnormal breathing patterns, or evidence of trauma
- Pallor, sweaty, nausea, palpitations
- Full recovery after a few minutes

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The differential for ALOC can be broad

**Consideration should be given to:**
  - Intracranial pathologies such as CVA, subarachnoid haemorrhage, intracerebral haemorrhage, meningitis, encephalitis, post-ictal, status epilepticus, Wernicke- Korsakoff, hypertensive encephalopathy
  - Extra-Cranial pathologies such as dys/arrythmia, blood glucose deficits, electrolyte imbalances, sedatives, opiate, or anticonvulsant medications, infection, sepsis, environmental conditions, hypoxia, substance abuse, substance/medication withdrawal, factitious or psychiatric disorder

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety, responder safety is paramount
- Primary Survey
- Consider patient positioning, spinal precautions if indicated
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey
- Manage reversible causes (e.g. blood glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, hypoxia, hypo/hyperthermia) if evident
- Consider naloxone in suspected opioid toxicity in line with local protocol
- Keep patient warm
- Secondary/CNS Survey, FAST, cranial nerve assessment
- Clinicians should have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor if there is no improvement with simple measures or if the cause remains unclear

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway where indicated
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Manage cardiac dysrhythmia
- A 12 lead ECG should be acquired in most, if not all these patients
- If indicated, gain vascular access
- Consider obtaining pre-hospital blood sample for i-STAT if time permits
- Consider IV fluids if indicated
- Consider IV naloxone, titrate to effect (no large boluses) in suspected opiate toxicity, if IM not previously given, in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider benzodiazepines (titrated to effect) if seizure activity evident in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - ALOC may fluctuate with time and response to treatment
